title: Fleece selection at the farm
subtitle: How to assess a raw fleece before buying: staple length, micron count, vegetable matter, and condition
excerpt: How to assess a raw fleece at the source: what to look for in staple length, micron count, lanolin content, vegetable matter, and overall fleece condition. Covers breed-specific expectations and the questions to ask a shepherd before buying.
sourceNotes: Fleece assessment criteria are consistent across UK and US spinning guild teaching. The descriptions of staple, micron, and vegetable matter draw on the general tradition of shepherd-to-spinner purchasing relationships. Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A natural cluster of fibres that grows together on the sheep and locks into a recognisable tuft in the shorn fleece. Staple length is measured from the skin-end to the tip of one cluster. Longer staple suits worsted spinning; shorter staple suits woollen spinning.

The unit used to measure fibre diameter. Finer fibres have lower micron counts and feel softer against the skin; coarser fibres have higher counts and are more durable. Merino typically measures 16 to 25 microns; coarser breeds measure 28 to 40 microns.

Seeds, hay, thistle burrs, and other plant material embedded in a raw fleece. Light vegetable matter falls out during washing and carding; heavy vegetable matter (especially seeds with barbs) is time-consuming to remove and may be impossible to pick out fully.

content.text: A raw fleece bought directly from a shepherd or at a wool fair is the cheapest way to acquire large quantities of fibre for spinning, dyeing, and felting. Quality varies enormously. A fleece from a well-kept flock shorn at the right time is clean, long-stapled, and easy to prepare. A fleece from an animal in poor condition is short, tender, and full of vegetable matter. Learning to assess a fleece before buying prevents disappointment.
Open the fleece and find a

in the middle of the blanket (the main body section). Measure from the skin-end cut to the tip. Most fleeces sold once a year have staple lengths between 7 and 15 cm depending on breed. For hand-spinning on a wheel, aim for a minimum of 7 cm; shorter than this and the fibre is difficult to draft cleanly. For spindle spinning, 5 to 7 cm is workable with practice. Very long staples (over 20 cm) suit worsted spinning and are useful for adding lustre to a blend.
If you do not have a micron meter (most spinners do not), rub a lock of the fleece against your neck for 30 seconds. Fibres below 25

will feel comfortable with no prickling; coarser fibres prickle noticeably. This neck test is not precise but it is reliable for separating next-to-skin soft from coarser outer-garment or accessory fibre. Ask the shepherd for the breed and the typical micron range for that breed as a cross-check.
Hold a lock up to the light and shake it gently. Fine chaff and dust will dislodge; what remains stuck is

. A small amount of hay chaff is normal and comes out in washing. Thistle seeds with hooked barbs, burr medick, and goose-grass all embed in fibres and are very difficult to remove. A lock where you can see multiple barbed seeds before you have even opened it is not worth purchasing unless the price reflects the extra processing time.

Tease a small section of the fleece apart with both hands and listen: a healthy fleece parts with a soft tearing sound. A tender fleece (one where the sheep experienced stress during the growing season, such as illness, poor nutrition, or difficult lambing) snaps cleanly with a sharp crack as the fibres break. Tender fleece is difficult to spin into strong yarn: the short break point means the yarn will pill and break in use.

When buying from a shepherd in person, ask the following. What breed is the animal? When was it shorn: a fleece over 18 months old may have weathered tips. Has the animal had any health issues in the last year? Is this a first clip from a young animal: first clips are often softer but shorter than later ones. A shepherd who keeps spinners on a regular buyer list will usually set aside their cleanest fleeces and notify you at shearing time.
